# Questions in Livestream 30

Live Stream #30: Full Moon on Saturday.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=244" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:04</a> | How did bulls become the go-to sacrifice in the ancient Near East, and could this connect to a Proto-Indo-European creation myth? | He said bulls mattered because cattle were economically central, so sacrificing one meant surrendering something valuable. He did not see a likely Proto-Indo-European connection because that myth probably came later. | He separates the Proto-Indo-European issue from the ancient Near Eastern pattern and says the former probably postdates the relevant Near Eastern evidence. Bulls became prominent sacrificial animals because cattle were central to agriculture, food, milk, meat, and family survival across the region. Since sacrifice is supposed to cost the worshiper something valuable, offering a cow or bull made sense as a serious economic surrender rather than a disposable gift. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=395" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:35</a> | How should claims about Canaanites in Egypt and Hoffmeier's motives be evaluated? | He said motive attacks are a distraction. The real issue is the historical connection between Canaanites in Avaris, the Exodus tradition, and later Israel in Canaan. | He calls the motive claim against Hoffmeier logically fallacious because it does not engage the evidence. The substantive question is whether the Canaanites at Avaris can be connected with the people who later appear as Israel in Canaan. He says a one-to-one proof is difficult because Israel becomes Israel after the Exodus, but the abandonment of Avaris, the Exodus account as the bridge, and the Merneptah Stele's later placement of Israel in Canaan form a consistent narrative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=705" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:45</a> | Does Jephthah's "300 years" statement in Judges 11 help date the Exodus? | He said not much. Because Jephthah is presented as an unreliable speaker in that passage, the number is weak evidence for Exodus chronology. | He says the statement is not useful for dating the Exodus because Judges presents Jephthah as an unreliable witness. Jephthah makes other theological and historical mistakes in the speech, including attributing land to Chemosh when Deuteronomy 32 says the Lord gives nations their land. Since both early-date and late-date interpreters generally place Jephthah around 1100 BC but interpret the 300 years differently, he thinks stronger evidence must come from elsewhere. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=1166" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:26</a> | What are his thoughts on Sargon of Akkad? | He rejected the idea that Moses is copied from Sargon, but said Sargon's story is important for river-culture child-abandonment traditions and as an archetype of the powerful usurper. | He rejects the claim that Moses is a copy of Sargon's birth story, noting that the earliest copies of the Sargon birth narrative are much later than the Exodus account. Still, he finds Sargon's story significant as a river-culture child-abandonment account and as an archetype of a powerful usurper. He connects Sargon's origin with a priestess breaking a vow, a basket on the river, and a later career as conqueror, which he relates to broader ancient Near Eastern fears about disruptive warrior figures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=1332" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:12</a> | How does he interpret the 430 years in Exodus 12:40 and Galatians 3:17, and does he favor a short or long sojourn? | He favored the short-sojourn reading, arguing that the Septuagint and Samaritan Pentateuch support counting time in both Canaan and Egypt. | He favors the short sojourn because the Septuagint and Samaritan Pentateuch read the 430 years as time in Egypt and Canaan, while the Masoretic Text has Egypt alone. In his view, the plurality of textual witnesses favors including both lands. That also fits Galatians 3:17, where the 430-year clock is connected with a promise to Abraham, even if Paul does not specify exactly which Abrahamic promise starts the count. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=1464" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:24</a> | Could Chedorlaomer be identified with a known historical king? | Possibly, but he said the identification is uncertain because more than one candidate name fits the relevant window. | He says the identification is possible, but the difficulty is that multiple similarly named kings fit the relevant window around Hammurabi. That makes it hard to single out one candidate as Chedorlaomer. He says campaign records from Elam would be especially helpful, but without more evidence the best answer is that one of the candidates may be right while certainty is not available. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=1559" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:59</a> | How should Exodus 21 on slaves, beatings, and "property" be understood? | He said the passage still reflects slavery, but also that Israelite law placed unusual limits on slaveowners compared with surrounding cultures and should not be flattened into later chattel-slavery assumptions. | He explains the "property" term as Hebrew `kesef`, whose basic sense is silver and which can function as a general property term. He says slavery in Israelite law should not be equated without qualification with later chattel slavery: if a slave converts to Yahweh worship or becomes part of the covenant community, the property aspect changes and release at Jubilee becomes relevant. He also stresses that Exodus 21 punishes a master if a slave dies, which he presents as an unusual legal limit compared with other ancient Near Eastern cultures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=1910" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:50</a> | What stands out about the Ain Dara temple? | He said the most memorable feature is the giant footprints at the entrance, a symbolic way of showing the deity entering the temple. | After reminding himself of the site, he identifies Ain Dara as a Syrian, probably Neo-Hittite, temple and says it is somewhat outside his main expertise. The feature he remembers is the large footprints at the entrance. He compares this to other temples such as Tel Dan and interprets the footprints as a symbolic sign that the god has entered the temple. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=2043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:03</a> | Did Solomon repent of idolatry and lust before his death? | He said scripture is not explicit, so certainty is impossible, but he leaned toward seeing Solomon as still among the righteous despite ending badly. | He says Scripture does not state clearly whether Solomon repented. Solomon began well, received miraculous wisdom, expanded the kingdom's influence, but later treaty wives turned his heart toward idolatry and high-place worship. He leans toward Solomon being among the righteous because the death notice says he "slept with his fathers," but he keeps the conclusion tentative because the text itself is not explicit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=2370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:30</a> | Does Exodus 3:6 show continued worship of God in Avaris similar to the patriarchs? | He said there is no evidence of Yahweh worship at Avaris; the phrase "God of your father" may refer to an ancestor such as Levi rather than proving continuity there. | He looks at the singular "your father" language and says it does not necessarily specify Moses' immediate father, since "father" can refer to a grandfather or more remote ancestor. He says Avaris has evidence for worship of Baal, Reshef, Astarte, and Egyptian gods, but no evidence for Yahweh worship. Therefore he reads the phrase as possibly pointing to an ancestor such as Levi rather than proving continued patriarchal-style Yahweh worship in Avaris. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=2583" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:03</a> | How long did Pithom last compared with Pi-Ramesses, and have ruins been found in the Wadi Tumilat? | He said Pithom is at Tell el-Retaba, lasted longer than Pi-Ramesses into the Third Intermediate Period, and is still producing excavation evidence. | He identifies Pithom with Tell el-Retaba and says ruins have been found there. He describes Pithom as earlier and longer-lived than Pi-Ramesses, beginning around Dynasty 11 and continuing into the Third Intermediate Period before ending around Dynasty 22. He also notes ongoing excavation and recent finds such as mudbrick architecture and the temple of Atum in the Wadi Tumilat region. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=2730" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:30</a> | Who was the queen of Sheba, where was Sheba, and was she related to Punt? | He said Punt was in eastern Africa, Sheba/Saba was in the Arabian peninsula, and the queen was likely acting as an ambassador in trade negotiations with Solomon. | He says Punt is in eastern Africa south of Nubia, while Sheba, or Saba, is in the Arabian peninsula and is not related to the queen of Punt. He presents the queen of Sheba as part of a diplomatic and trade mission to Israel. Since Solomon controlled key trade routes through the Fertile Crescent and taxed caravans, she likely functioned as an ambassador seeking favorable terms for incense trade. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=2889" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:09</a> | Could Avaris have been named after the biblical Joseph? | He rejected the etymology, saying the name does not work linguistically and Avaris was already an established Middle Kingdom city before Joseph's time. | He rejects the proposal because the etymology does not work, including problems with the relevant sounds, and because Avaris is itself a later form of an Egyptian name that he recalls as meaning something like "the two gates." He also says the city predates Joseph: Avaris goes back to the Middle Kingdom, around Dynasty 11, and was already established by the time Joseph would enter the story in a later dynasty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=3044" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:44</a> | What is the Near Eastern context of Genesis 38:8-10? | He explained it through levirate marriage: Onan's sin was refusing to preserve his brother's line and thereby violating clan integrity. | He explains Genesis 38 through levirate marriage, where an unmarried brother was expected to marry his deceased brother's widow and produce a first child counted as the dead brother's heir. He says Onan's sin is not self-pleasure but the deliberate refusal to preserve his brother's posterity. In that context, Onan is tearing at the integrity of the clan of Judah, which he describes as one of the gravest kinds of betrayal in ancient Near Eastern clan society. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=3283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:43</a> | What were forges and kilns fired with if wood was so expensive? | He said Egyptians could use reeds, chaff, dung, scraps, and wood from controlled regions such as Sinai or Kush, while large timber remained costly. | He says many kilns could be fired with alternatives to large timber, including dried reeds, hay, agricultural chaff, dung, scrub species, and burnable scraps. Some high-temperature smelting operations still required wood, but those often happened on Egypt's periphery, such as Sinai or Buhen, where acacia, Kushite wood, or imported resources were available. Large planks and logs remained expensive and were usually reserved for elite or funerary uses, but fuel-grade remains could still be burned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=3486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:06</a> | Does he think Ramesses III was assassinated? | Yes. He cited the harem conspiracy evidence and the mummy's throat wound as reasons he thinks assassination is now effectively settled. | He says yes, citing two lines of evidence. The harem conspiracy papyrus records the trial of people involved in a plot against Ramesses III, although it does not by itself prove whether the plot killed him. The mummy evidence, however, shows a throat incision that he describes as pre-mortem rather than a mummification cut, so he thinks the question is now effectively settled and widely accepted in Egyptology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=3627" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:27</a> | Could characters in Homer's Iliad be tribes the way Adam was a tribe? | He said he did not know Greek culture well enough to answer confidently, but thought Greek polity looked less tribal than ancient Near Eastern clan structures. | He explicitly says he does not know 13th-century Greek culture well enough to answer confidently. His tentative reaction is that Greek political organization looks more like small kingdoms, island states, and city-states than the tribal or clan patterns familiar from Semitic and ancient Near Eastern contexts. He allows that tribes may have existed among Greeks, but he does not want to claim more than he knows. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=3755" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:35</a> | How does ancient Near Eastern temple design compare with early Catholic or Orthodox basilica design? | He said he could speak mainly from Egyptian examples, where early church layouts seem more Roman or Hellenistic than Jewish or broadly Near Eastern. | He limits his answer to Egyptian examples he has seen from the Coptic era. Egyptian temples have a holiness structure centered on the innermost sacred space, with decreasing holiness outward, while the early church examples he describes do not preserve that same concentric pattern. He notes east-west orientation and front semi-dome or quarter-dome features in churches at places such as Dendera and Luxor, and he reads those design features as more Roman or Hellenistic than Egyptian, Jewish, or broadly Near Eastern. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=4007" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:47</a> | Was there already water in the rock that God commanded Moses to speak to in Numbers 20:11? | He thought the water was probably already there as a spring, but God intended Moses to reveal it in a way that displayed God's goodness rather than Moses' anger. | He says the text does not directly state whether water was already present, but the scene looks like a spring that God caused to gush forth. His interpretation is that God intended Moses to reveal the water so Israel would see God's goodness and learn faith. Moses instead used the event to express anger, thereby betraying the message and misusing God's word, which explains why he was barred from entering the land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=4203" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:03</a> | Are there good books on the history of the modern Messianic Jewish movement, and does it date directly to first-century Jewish Christians? | He did not have a book recommendation and said modern Messianic Judaism is not a direct institutional descendant, though Jews have converted to Christianity throughout church history. | He says he does not personally know a good book recommendation, though he knows people who might. He describes modern Messianic Judaism as a late 20th- and early 21st-century phenomenon rather than a direct descendant of first-century Jewish Christianity. At the same time, he stresses that Jews and even groups of Jews have converted to Christianity throughout church history, so the modern movement is a new blossoming rather than a total novelty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=4426" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:46</a> | Did demonic possession appear suddenly with Christ, or was there a concept of possession before Christ? | He said possession did not appear suddenly; it is found in Second Temple literature before Christ and continued in early Christian ministry after Christ. | He says demonic possession did not begin suddenly with Christ. It appears in Second Temple literature and in other accounts hundreds of years before Christ, even if it is not prominent in the Hebrew Bible itself. He also notes that Gospel cases include people afflicted before Jesus' public ministry and that early Christian writers such as Tertullian still describe exorcism as part of Christian ministry after Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=4588" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:28</a> | What about the theory that Moses was assassinated because he failed to enter the promised land, and why is his burial place unknown? | He dismissed the assassination theory as unsupported and said the unknown grave likely prevented veneration and ancestor-worship problems. | He dismisses the assassination theory as unsupported conjecture. For the unknown burial place, he argues that Israel was close to falling back into paganism and ancestor worship, and Moses would have known from Egypt how dead leaders could become objects of veneration. In his view, the grave was kept unknown so it would not become a shrine, temple, or stumbling block for Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=4812" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:12</a> | Does Deuteronomy 1:37 contradict Numbers 20:11 on why Moses could not enter the promised land? | He said no; after checking the sequence, he connected the broader Deuteronomy statement with Numbers 32 and treated Moses' own exclusion as already grounded in Numbers 20. | He works through the sequence and says the passages do not contradict one another. Numbers 20 concerns Moses specifically and grounds his exclusion in the rock incident, while Deuteronomy speaks in a broader context of the generation's exclusion. After checking cross-references, he connects Deuteronomy 1:37 with Numbers 32 and concludes that Moses' disqualification in Numbers 20 comes before the broader statement about the people. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=5283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:03</a> | Did ancient people cultivate forestry to secure wood resources, or did military activity damage long-term agricultural endeavors? | He said ancient Egypt does not appear to have practiced silviculture in that way, and exploitation of forest resources likely damaged some areas long term. | He says ancient Egypt does not appear to have practiced responsible silviculture or forest cultivation in the modern sense, though ancient societies did secure forests for wood resources. He mentions Japan as an example of older deliberate forestry but says most premodern states did not do this. Egypt's exploitation of forest resources in areas such as Kush likely damaged those regions' long-term ability to support trees, while modern Egyptian projects to green desert regions are a separate contemporary development. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=5558" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:38</a> | Which language is closer to Middle Egyptian, modern Coptic or Nubian? | He said modern Coptic is closest, though it is nearly dead as a living language and differs in orthography and its own later features. | He answers that modern Coptic is the closest modern language to Egyptian, with Coptic especially close to Late Egyptian in dialect. He qualifies that modern Coptic has its own idiosyncrasies, uses different orthography from Middle Egyptian hieroglyphs, and is nearly dead as a living language, being spoken by only a very small number of families. Even with those qualifications, he says it is as close as a modern language gets. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=5644" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:04</a> | Would he debate Dr. James White on autonomy and hermeneutics related to Exodus 9? | Probably not. He said he generally finds debates unproductive and would rather teach and do research unless a debate serves a very specific purpose. | He says he does not really like debates and would rather teach and do research. He agrees with Michael Heiser's view that debates are usually a waste of time because they tend to confirm people on their existing side rather than change minds. He allows that debates can be useful in rare cases, especially when they expose that a participant does not understand their own view, but he does not see a productive purpose in this proposed debate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=5868" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:48</a> | What does he think of Mark S. Smith's argument that Deuteronomy 32:8-9 shows Old Testament religion was polytheistic? | He rejected the argument, saying the literary structure reads as God dividing the nations while reserving Israel for himself, not as Elyon giving Israel to a separate Yahweh. | He reads Deuteronomy 32:8-9 as poetic and structurally patterned, not as a story of one high god assigning Israel to a separate Yahweh. In his reading, the Lord divides the nations and their inheritances while reserving Jacob or Israel as his own special portion. He connects this back to Judges 11, saying Jephthah was wrong to say Chemosh gave land, because Deuteronomy 32 teaches that God gives nations their land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=6302" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:02</a> | Did the Israelites have board games, or were they too busy surviving? | He said probably yes, citing Levantine evidence such as the Megiddo ivories, game boards, dice, and throwing sticks. | He says the answer is probably yes, especially if the question is about board games. He points to the Megiddo ivories, including a small semi-circular ivory board with holes and pegs that appears to have been used with dice-like objects. He also notes broader Levantine evidence for dice, throwing sticks, and game boards, so even if Israelite examples have not survived directly, the surrounding cultural evidence supports the likelihood. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=6551" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:11</a> | If a creationist is someone who believes Yahweh created the world, how do his views differ from old-earth creationism? | He said creationism is usually more specifically about creation ex nihilo, but he currently does not identify with young-earth, old-earth, evolutionary, or theistic-evolutionary positions. | He says "creationist" usually means more than believing Yahweh created the world; classically it involves creation ex nihilo, with young-earth and old-earth views differing over when and how that occurs. He has exegetical reservations about treating Genesis 1:1 as both a summary and a specific creative act at the same time. He also stresses that he is not taking an evolutionist or theistic-evolutionist position either, but is withholding judgment because he thinks the textual and scientific questions both require more careful work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=7041" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:21</a> | Is there a book he would recommend for biblical geography, and would he sign a copy of his book? | He recommended Barry Beitzel's atlas and another atlas by Alan Millard and Paul Lawrence, and said he would be happy to sign the book by arrangement. | He recommends two atlas-style resources for biblical geography: Barry Beitzel's atlas, which he says was his own course textbook and works well as a standalone resource, and an atlas by Alan Millard and Paul Lawrence. On the signing question, he says he would be happy and honored to sign a copy if the person contacts him and arranges a meeting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=7219" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:19</a> | What is his favorite archaeological find, and what does he hope gets found in his lifetime? | His favorite was Tutankhamun's tomb. He hoped for finds such as the tomb of Imhotep, Sukkot, Pi-hahiroth, Baal-zephon, or a plausible tomb of Joseph. | He names Tutankhamun's tomb as his favorite discovery because it transformed Egyptology, biblical studies, and understanding of royal burial practice through its intact texts, artifacts, shrines, and funerary context. For future discoveries, he hopes for the tomb of Imhotep, likely somewhere around Saqqara, and for clearer identifications of Exodus-route places such as Sukkot, Pi-hahiroth, and Baal-zephon. He also says a plausible tomb of Joseph would be interesting, but rejects David Rohl's proposed tomb because he thinks Joseph's body would not have survived burial below the water table. |
